packs.name: rsync
packs.description: Protects against destructive rsync operations like --delete and its variants.

destructive_patterns.name: rsync-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: rsync --delete removes destination files not present in source.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
The --delete flag removes files from the destination that don't exist in the source. If source and destination are swapped, or if the source is unexpectedly empty, important data on the destination can be permanently deleted.

Safer alternatives:
- rsync --dry-run --delete: Preview what would be deleted
- rsync without --delete: Only adds/updates, never deletes
- rsync --backup --delete: Keep backup copies of deleted files

destructive_patterns.name: rsync-del-short
destructive_patterns.reason: rsync --del is a short alias for --delete and is destructive.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
The --del flag is shorthand for --delete-during, which deletes destination files not present in source during the transfer. This is slightly more dangerous than --delete-after because deletions happen incrementally.

Safer alternatives:
- rsync --dry-run --del: Preview deletions first
- rsync without deletion flags: Additive sync only
- Use --delete-after for predictable batch deletion

packs.name: ssh
packs.description: Protects against destructive SSH operations like remote command execution and key management.

destructive_patterns.name: ssh-remote-rm-rf
destructive_patterns.reason: SSH remote execution contains destructive rm -rf command.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Executing rm -rf on a remote system via SSH can cause irreversible data loss. Remote systems often have different directory structures and you may not have the same safety mechanisms in place.

Safer alternatives:
- SSH in and run commands interactively with confirmation
- Use --dry-run or preview flags when available
- Ensure backups exist on the remote system

destructive_patterns.name: ssh-remote-git-reset-hard
destructive_patterns.reason: SSH remote execution contains destructive git reset --hard command.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Running git reset --hard on a remote server discards all uncommitted changes. On production servers, this might destroy deployment state or configuration changes made directly on the server.

Safer alternatives:
- git stash on the remote first to save changes
- Use git status remotely to check for uncommitted work
- Consider proper deployment pipelines instead of direct remote git

destructive_patterns.name: ssh-remote-git-clean
destructive_patterns.reason: SSH remote execution contains destructive git clean -f command.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Running git clean -f on a remote server permanently removes untracked files. This might delete logs, uploads, or configuration files that were never committed to the repository.

Safer alternatives:
- Run git clean -n first to preview what would be deleted
- Use .gitignore to protect important untracked files
- Back up untracked files before cleaning

destructive_patterns.name: ssh-keygen-remove-host
destructive_patterns.reason: ssh-keygen -R removes entries from known_hosts file.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Removing entries from known_hosts weakens protection against man-in-the-middle attacks. The next connection will trust any key presented by the remote host.

Safer alternatives:
- Verify the new host key fingerprint before removing old entry
- Use ssh-keyscan to preview the new key
- Update entry rather than removing (add new key, then remove old)

destructive_patterns.name: ssh-add-delete-all
destructive_patterns.reason: ssh-add -d/-D removes identities from the SSH agent.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Removing SSH identities from the agent will require re-authentication for subsequent connections. Using -D removes ALL identities, which may interrupt active sessions or scripts.

Safer alternatives:
- Use -d to remove specific keys rather than -D for all
- List keys with ssh-add -l before removing
- Re-add keys immediately if needed

destructive_patterns.name: ssh-remote-sudo-rm
destructive_patterns.reason: SSH remote execution with sudo rm is high-risk.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Executing sudo rm on a remote system bypasses normal permission restrictions and can delete system files. Combined with SSH, there's no interactive confirmation and errors may not be visible.

Safer alternatives:
- SSH in and run sudo commands interactively
- Use mv to a backup location instead of rm
- Implement proper cleanup scripts with safety checks

packs.name: scp
packs.description: Protects against destructive SCP operations like overwrites to system paths.

destructive_patterns.name: scp-recursive-root
destructive_patterns.reason: scp -r to root (/) is extremely dangerous.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Recursive copy to the root filesystem can overwrite critical system files, potentially rendering the system unbootable. This affects all system directories including /etc, /bin, /lib, and /boot.

Safer alternatives:
- Specify a target subdirectory instead of /
- Use rsync with --dry-run to preview changes
- Copy to /tmp first and move files individually

destructive_patterns.name: scp-to-etc
destructive_patterns.reason: scp to /etc/ can overwrite system configuration.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
The /etc directory contains critical system configuration files including passwd, shadow, fstab, and network settings. Overwriting these can lock you out of the system or cause services to fail.

Safer alternatives:
- Copy to a staging directory first
- Back up existing files before overwriting
- Use configuration management tools (Ansible, etc.)

destructive_patterns.name: scp-to-var
destructive_patterns.reason: scp to /var/ can overwrite system data.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
The /var directory contains variable data including logs, databases, mail spools, and application state. Overwriting this data can cause data loss and service disruptions.

Safer alternatives:
- Use /var/tmp for temporary staging
- Stop affected services before modifying their data
- Back up existing data first

destructive_patterns.name: scp-to-boot
destructive_patterns.reason: scp to /boot/ can corrupt boot configuration.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
The /boot directory contains the kernel, initramfs, and bootloader configuration. Corrupting these files will prevent the system from booting, requiring rescue media to recover.

Safer alternatives:
- Use package manager for kernel updates
- Keep backup kernels available
- Test changes in a VM first

destructive_patterns.name: scp-to-usr
destructive_patterns.reason: scp to /usr/ can overwrite system binaries.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
The /usr directory contains system binaries, libraries, and shared resources. Overwriting files here can break system utilities and installed applications.

Safer alternatives:
- Use /usr/local for custom installations
- Use package managers for system updates
- Install to user directories when possible

destructive_patterns.name: scp-to-bin
destructive_patterns.reason: scp to /bin/ or /sbin/ can overwrite system binaries.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
The /bin and /sbin directories contain essential system binaries required for basic operation. Overwriting these can make the system unusable and require rescue mode recovery.

Safer alternatives:
- Install custom scripts to /usr/local/bin
- Use package managers for system updates
- Test binaries in user directories first

destructive_patterns.name: scp-to-lib
destructive_patterns.reason: scp to /lib/ can overwrite system libraries.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
The /lib and /lib64 directories contain shared libraries required by system binaries. Overwriting these can cause immediate system instability and prevent commands from running.

Safer alternatives:
- Use package managers for library updates
- Install custom libraries to /usr/local/lib
- Use LD_LIBRARY_PATH for testing
